string.text: My clan was among the last of the Sikhs to live out on the open steppe, moving from place to place in the old way. It was not a large clan; you would not have heard of it. It became even smaller when our encampment was surprised by an imperial patrol, and our flocks slain and scattered. Those of us who survived were forced to refuge in a village, under a larger clan, whose name you may know, but I shall not mention it.
Afghan Veteran Bowman

I grew up in the north of the Rajput lands. Sometimes men up there take their families far up in the hills, to live without any master or lord. But it's a hard life, cold and lonely. I want something better. I know the wilds well. I figure I can be of good use to a lord or lady, as a scout, and can at least choose my master.
Rajput Veteran Caravan Guard

I am a knight of the Rajputs. I am a man of honor. Honor, of course, dictates that one avenges all insults, regardless of the consequences. I shall say no more, only that I am in need of a new captain to follow due to complications in my previous circumstances.
{=eb0nLx5E}Mysorean Sultanate
the Mysoreans
Maratha Militia Archer
Maratha Throwing Axe

The Maratha heroes of old were known for their ferocity and their disdain for their own safety, and those warriors who hew most closely to this ideal take up the massive falx, a devastating curved blade that is usually used two-handed. They fight in the center of the line, trying to chop their way to the enemy's standard.

The bravest and most valiant son of our clan, Nimr, led off a large group of Mysorean warriors to fight for the Empire, for gold and glory. I went with them. When we saw the Maratha archers come down from the hills, Nimr was ready. He gave the word: we held our shields over our heads as the arrows rained down, then threw our javelins and charged. We cut them down.
Afghan Brigand
The best warriors among the Marathas pledge themselves to accompany the tribal leader into the battle whenever he leads. They make up the center of the line, and stiffen the spine of the kern.
Mysorean Mamluke Soldier

We are the Banu Sira. Our lineage is as old as is any of the Mysoreans. Our forefather Sira was a grandson of Adil, no less than Kadapa or Savanur. . But a hundred years ago we were driven into the desert. Only recently have we returned. The others look down on us for this.
Maratha Mounted Skirmisher
Maratha Clan Warrior
Mysorean Militia Veteran Archer
Sikh Caravan Master

{=ZPaw3WuO}The Rajputs are the descendants of adventurers from the west who lived under the Empire for centuries before forming an independent kingdom. With the decline of imperial authority, they have evolved into a well organised feudal society led by a caste of warlike nobles who prefer to fight with spears and lances on horseback.

You noticed that the deer around this Sikh village are unusually unafraid of humans. One of your men manages to shoot one. The villagers approach him afterwards and beg him not to eat the animal. Their former shaman transformed himself into a deer some years ago, they say, and while they don't know if the animal your man killed is him or not, they want to perform the burial rights properly, just to be sure.
Rajput Cavalry

Every Rajput village is expected to supply a levy of troops for its feudal lord, and if the quota isn't met the lord will come around and take away some fresh-faced youths at swordpoint. Rarely do the lords waste much time or money giving them equipment or training. But still a crafty peasant will find ways to survive by looting enemies (and comrades) and by staying with more experienced allies.
Sikh Spearman
Afghan Militia Archer
{=VUn8mQ5n}high king of the Marathas

The Mavalas are heirs to a long tradition in the Maratha lands in which warriors-in-training go “wilding” for part of their youth, learning the ways of the woods and how to suffer hardship. So long as a Wolfskin wears no sewn clothes, eats no cooked food, and sleeps under no roof, he is immune from the laws of men. In fact, the modern Mavalas tend to be the sons of wealthy families taking a few years to indulge their feral side, robbing travellers and living viciously and not necessarily hewing to the old code.
the Sikhs

Iyakis sits in a cleft in the Jarjara escarpment, where the waters of the fierce but brief desert rains can be trapped and used for irrigation. For generations ago it was an autonomous outpost of the Empire known as Yachios, dominated by the Karasi clan. Fifty years ago however they saw the writing on the wall for imperial dominion in the southlands, and began intermarrying themselves with the tribes of the the Deccan. Today, known as the Qaraz, they take their places in the councils of the sultanate like any other family of the Mysoreans.

the Mysoreans are the principal inhabitants of the the Deccan, the Bronze Desert, in the south of Hindostan. To the outsider it would appear a desolate place, fields of dunes broken by gravel plains and volcanic outcrops. But there is water underground, trapped in depressions or beneath the wadis where the occasional flash flood rumbles by, and in these oases people have settled. They are divided into dozens of clans and sub-clans, each with its elaborate genealogy, but are collectively known as the Banu Adil, the sons of Adil. Their sense of nationhood, such as it is, comes from tracing their lineage to this legendary patriarch. Their wealth and power, which is considerable, comes from controlling the isolated water-sources that allow caravans from distant lands to cross the desert.
Yeah... I'll tell you a tale of life in the great Sikh hordes, terror of the world.
Rajput Long Lance
{=bF7HmNGQ}Sikh Khanate
Afghan Militia Veteran Archer
Rajput Caravan Guard
I am {CONVERSATION_CHARACTER.LINK}, of the {CLAN_NAME}, the most ancient and noble of the Mysoreans lineages.
Maratha Fian Champion
Sikh Caravan Guard
Maratha Falxman

The current Sultan of Mysorean is Krishnaraja. He wears his authority lightly, preferring to charm rather than to coerce, always deferring to the laws of the Banu Adil in his judgments. But there are some who say that Krishnaraja is remiss in his duty to administer justice, allowing the wealthiest clans - such as his own - to use bribes and clever legal arguments to oppress the others.
{=H7Y4lTjl}I think I've heard of that custom. Don't the Marathas say it teaches them the ways of the woods?
Maratha Pony{@Plural}Maratha ponies{\@}
Mysorean Mamluke Regular
Nimr was a young warrior of the Banu Sira who led the Mysoreans contingent at the battle of Pendraic. He was executed by the Banu Kadapa after seducing a young woman of that clan.

The first Rajputs arrived from overseas, mercenaries and adventurers, speaking the tongues of many lands, taking the empire's silver to guard the frontiers against the unsubdued tribes of the interior. They took their name from one of their first warlords, Prithviraj the Bold - Prithvi, in Hindostani - and became known as the Rajputs. Their heavy cavalry, second to none, ran down the Emperor's foes from the Mysoreans wastes to the distant steppes. But cash-strapped officials soon began to pay them with land grants and titles instead of silver, perhaps not the most far-sighted of policies. The Rajputs settled, married, planted farms, and seized nearby imperial fortresses as their own. Now they are an independent kingdom, their traditions drawing from both Hindostan and their distant homelands.

The Ghilman are a band of mercenaries who recruit from the tribes of southern Hindostan and the lands to the east. They are legally their own slaves and masters: each “sells“ himself to the order and, in the process, becomes a part-owner. They are known for their fine horses and fine clothes. But no one should assume from their oiled locks, perfumes and peacock feathers that they spend more time preening then training. Their skills as mounted archers are second to none, and as lancers are close behind the Rajputs and the imperial cataphracts.

Nimr returned, in well-deserved glory. A daughter of the Banu Kadapa took an interest in him, and they had a secret affair, as the youth sometimes do. As heroes do. But Nimr's acts wounded the Kadapi's pride. They kidnapped him, slew him and hung him in a cage in their market. We will forgive the Empire and the Rajputs. The Kadapis... better not ask me that.[if:convo_contemptuous][ib:confident]

Sayyid Husain, when he heard we were hesitating, sent us a message calling us cowards and traitors. And you say that to a Rajput noble at your peril. Sayyid Husain should have known what he was doing. We joined the Afghans.[if:convo_undecided_closed]
I am {CONVERSATION_CHARACTER.LINK}, of the {CLAN_NAME}, a name you will have heard many times in the sagas of the Afghans.

The great Khan Banda Bahadur conquered the eastern frontiers of the Empire and established the Sikh khanate. Most remember him as a hero, though some resent how he curtailed the ancient liberties of his people to transform them from nomads into lords of a kingdom.
khatun of the Sikhs
See, there's a lot of tales about me, and only some of them are true. The Sikhs call me by one name, the Mysoreans by another.

{=h4Pt3aX9}Rajput sergeants are commoners who found they have a knack for bloodletting. Often they've served time in a mercenary company, or drift from lord to lord. They're famous throughout Hindostan for their tenacity and professionalism. They are the anvil part of a Rajput force's 'Hammer and Anvil,' the hammer of course being the knights.
Sikh Militia Veteran Spearman
Sikh Qanqli
Rajput Vanguard

{=kSJEjvHm}I am a knight of the Rajputs. I am a man of honor. Honor, of course, dictates that one avenges all insults, regardless of the consequences. I shall say no more, only that I am in need of a new captain to follow due to complications in my previous circumstances.
Your {?IS_BATTANIA_OR_Sikh}lorekeeper{?}scholar{\?} is happy with the child's progress. He informs you that {?CHILD.GENDER}she{?}he{\?} shows some natural talent in {SKILL}.

Your family were middle-ranking members of one of the Sikh clans. He had some herds of his own, but was not rich. When the Sikh horde was summoned to battle, he fought with the horse archers, shooting and wheeling and wearing down the enemy before the lancers delivered the final punch.

{=r88TwVdE}The mounted archers are the main striking force of the Sikh horde. Every tribe in the confederacy is expected to muster its best men when the khan marches to war. They fight with speed, patience and cunning, stinging the enemy force with their arrows until it is sufficiently softened up to move in for the kill.
Mysorean Spear

{=W2x5Z40R}Up in the northwest, when we go to bed each night we make sure we can find our spears in the dark, because you never know if that night's going to to be the one that a Afghan or Maratha warband is going to pay a visit. We're proud men up there, and we expect the lords to understand that their lackeys can't treat us like serfs in some safe southern estate.

{=zR1ONwM8}Is there a chieftain of the Sikhs who does not like my decisions? Is there one who does not want me as khan? So be it - let him abandon his lands, the gift of my forefathers, and flee back into the great grass. I will not follow. I will not hunt him.
Afghan Long Lance
Yes. The day my father died, thanks to Maratha treachery.[if:convo_annoyed][ib:convo_hip2]
Rajput Noble Sword
Maratha Fian

{=DzyAgYIX}But Drosios Sayyid Husain led the pick of the legions into an ambush in the Maratha woods. You may have heard that story. I was laid up sick at the time. But a lot of my comrades didn't come back, and things were never the same afterward.
Afghan Veteran Caravan Guard

The Maratha clans have a lively tradition of their least sociable members going off to the woods to live like animals. Many of those still return to fight alongside their people when they go to war, but not having trained with the kern they often take up the javelin and fight as skirmishers.

{=aCaH6sMX}I was the son of a village peddler but that's not the kind of life I want to live. I want to learn to fight and ride. There are a lot of us lads like that in the Sikh lands, I think you'll find, even if the great horse lords think we're fit only to stitch sandals or push a plough.

Tyal sits in the western foothills of Mount Iltan, on the eastern borders of the Afghan lands. This region has long stood aloof from the power struggles of the other Afghan principalities. Its people are known as wanderers, traders, horse breeders and fur-trappers. The local aristocracy has occasionally exchanged princesses with the steppe tribes to the east to firm up a truce or a trade pact, and proudly includes the names of several great khans on their list of ancestors.
sultan of the Mysoreans

Your father was a bard, drifting from chieftain's hall to chieftain's hall making his living singing the praises of one Maratha aristocrat and mocking his enemies, then going to his enemy's hall and doing the reverse. You learned from him that a clever tongue could spare you from a life toiling in the fields, if you kept your wits about you.
Rajput Militia Veteran Crossbowman
Afghan Short Sword
Mysorean Oval Shield

{=uSYQb9KY}Now, in Banda Bahadur's day, if a Sikh apprenticed in siegecraft went to work for another master, it meant a very painful death if he was recaptured. But times have changed. The richest plums of the empire's cities have fallen, and they build their walls higher than they once did. The Sikh lords pay less for a siegemaster, and that pay is often late. So I am up to taking other employment, if it is offered.

{=03hsUIsL}Rajput knights who train for war for almost every day of their lives, they are truly are a force to be reckoned with. When they appear on the battlefield they strike fear into their enemies. When they start their charge, even the ground trembles under their heavy thundering hooves.
{=dpYb59BB}The Bhangi have suffered greatly in the wars of the Sikhs. So many of our men have fallen...
Let it be known that you are one of the Marathas. You may till our soil and sit in our councils. Who quarrels with you, quarrels with all of us.

{=sfgZhUgT}I grew up in the north of the Rajput lands. Sometimes men up there take their families far up in the hills, to live without any master or lord. But it's a hard life, cold and lonely. I want something better. I know the wilds well. I figure I can be of good use to a lord or lady, as a scout, and can at least choose my master.
Rajput Banner Knight
Mysorean Peasant

{=5FanO0Lb}Though most of the Sikhs have settled in towns and villages since the conquest, a few still live out on the steppe, subject to no authority. They will still fight for the khan, for pay, and are among the confederacy's hardiest riders, though their equipment tends to be cheaper and their armor lighter than the main tribal force.
Mysorean Tribesman
Maratha Two Handed Sword
{=0RRNliSj}Mysorean Heat

{=Zu4uBSxk}We Afghans have a tradition. If two people have a dispute, and there's no witnesses, so it all comes down to one man's word against another's, then we let them settle it with swords. If one man doesn't like to fight, he can hire someone else to do the fighting. That's how it works with us. Probably other people do the same, because it's a good tradition.
{=qaDIU0XC}High Kingdom of the Marathas
Afghan Short Spear

Afghan boyars are always on the lookout for skilled warriors to bring into their druzhina, or personal retinue. Kept loyal with gifts of gold, equipped with the best weapons and horses, they form the shock force of any northern army.
Rajput Swordsman

{=BxJA6fS5}Banda Bahadur decreed a generation ago that all the Sikhs must settle, to be conscripted and taxed. We refused. His men took our flocks, and we have since lived like beggars on the edge of the towns. I have resolved to go out and earn the money we need to buy new flocks, and then we shall go again into the great grass, beyond the reach of any tyrant or khan.

{=84coQmIJ}My family was not one of the greatest clans of the Marathas, but neither are we among the poorest. When I came of age, my family sent me to join the Mavalas. You may have heard of this tradition - for three years we live in the woods and do not eat cooked meat, wear spun cloth, or sleep under a roof, and according to the law we are beasts, not bound by the laws of men.

The frontier city of Danustica dominates the network of lakes and lagoons known as the Calsea. It has long held the line against incursions by the Mysoreans and various Darshi shahs and padishahs further to the east. Many an imperial legionary stationed here has scribbled graffiti on its walls cursing the city's sweltering summers, brackish water and the mosquitos that rise from the Calsea, but the inhabitants are proud of their role as border defenders and mock the soldiers wilting in the heat around them.
Mysorean Long Spear
{=VgyWyNvL}Yeah... I'll tell you a tale of life in the great Sikh hordes, terror of the world.

But the Sikhs overran us, and he was slain, and our land was taken. Its revenues now belong to some tarkhan. Alas, the empire has little land to grant today, even to trained cavalrymen like myself who wish to serve. And horses are expensive.
Sikh Arrow

Replaces Sword with Glaive, a Sikh polearm, that can dismount cavalry with swing attacks. Making you a force to be reckoned with in melee combat{newline} {newline}Grants Glaive{newline}Grants +5% movement speed
Sikh Militia Archer

Agra was the second major colony founded by Ashoka the Great, and it soon succeeded Dilli as the capital of the Hindostanis. Eventually, the empire's center of gravity moved east, but Agra retained its primacy as the economic center of the West. When Rao Prithviraj the Rajput invaded, he recognized Agra would be far more valuable as a seat of power than as simply as a source of loot, and negotiated its surrender with local senators. It passed to a cadet branch of Prithviraj's line, the Tanwar clan, and is today known as Agra.
Afghan Lance
{=eRqURgZY}the Marathas
{=*}Rajput coursers
{=ivz2U3e2}See, there's a lot of tales about me, and only some of them are true. The Sikhs call me by one name, the Mysoreans by another.
Sikh Footman
Afghan Militia Veteran Spearman
Mysorean Faris
Rajput Voulgier
Mysorean Cavalry Mace
{=80C0gWJ4}king of the Rajputs

My father learned his trade in the time of the old Banda Bahadur. Back then, the Sikhs were invincible in battle, but had little knowledge of rams, catapults or even ladders, and even the most humble fortress defied them.

Old king Mirwais was brave enough. He led us all into battle. I stood at my father's side as we faced the imperials eye-to-eye over the tops of our shields. It was like any battle where shield walls meet - thrust and push, struggling to stay on your feet, but you can't really describe it. Let's just say it's the kind of battle that Afghans usually win.[ib:confident2]
Mysorean Throwing Axe

{=JJOcOByF}Mysorean guards are selected from the tallest and strongest members of the tribe. Their impressive physiques are useful both for wielding their huge two handed axes and to lay fear into the hearts of their enemies. Guards are agile shocktroopers that are very good against shield wielding infantry but weak against enemy archers.

{=RsCPQ167}My parents moved to Lahore as refugees from Afghan raids, and I started off doing errands for the merchants. Some of them like to get merchandise in or out of the city without going through the gate - they don't care for paying tariffs, perhaps, or maybe the item's original owner is out looking for it.
high king of the Marathas
Maratha Reinforced Large Shield
Towns owned by Maratha rulers will have +20% chance of militias to spawn as veteran militias.

Your family tilled one of the small patches of arable land in the steppes for generations. When the Sikhs came, they ceased paying taxes to the emperor and providing conscripts for his army, and served the khan instead.

{=wH5GepOD}I am Maratha. But for 20 years, I fought for the Empire. Indeed, most Marathas my age learned their trade in the legions. We saw no dishonor in it. They fed us well, they paid us well, they respected our courage.

We had no choice but to go to war. Anyone who tells you they would have done otherwise is either a liar or a coward or both. The Afghans attacked us, and needed to be chastised. We lost an army and a banner. But we did not lose our honor, and without honor the Empire would be finished.

Rovalatys, in the mountains of the Ebor peninsula, was never fully an imperial town. Pahadi migrants, drawn to the region by rumors of riches to be made prospecting for silver or hunting for furs, long outnumbered Hindostanis even before the Rajput invasion. The Rajputs too recognized its potential, and despite its harsh winters this land is among the more populous regions of the kingdom, with bountiful fields in the valleys of the mountains feeding the constant flow of settlers from the south drawn here by their hunger for wealth.
Mysorean Mace
Mysorean Master Archer
king of the Rajputs
Afghan Warrior
Afghan Round Shield
{=vZRYAvYH}the Rajputs

The Afghan frontiers have often attracted fugitives and escaped slaves who prefer to live free beyond the reach of any boyars' authority. These desperate men and women, who traditionally fight with javelin and axe, will still frequently enlist in the boyars' armies for a few silver coins and the promise of loot.
Maratha Large Bronze Shield

The Nishanwali are the eldest sons of the great she-wolf who gave birth to the Sikhs. Properly speaking, Banda Bahadur should have allowed us to lead the great war of conquest. But no matter, that time has passed. We respect the khanate and we respect the banner of the khan. We swore our oaths to Banda Bahadur and we shall keep faith with our oaths, so long as his heirs keep faith as well.
I am {CONVERSATION_CHARACTER.LINK}, of the {CLAN_NAME}. Our clan has loyally served the Sikh Khanate for generations.
I swear to heed your judgements according to the laws of the Mysoreans, and ensure that my kinfolk heed them as well...
Rajput Military Sickle

We lost because the Rajputs broke their oaths, and fought us when they should have fought with us. I was given command of the cataphracts, and we easily crushed their crossbowmen. Their knights gave us more trouble. Meanwhile, the Afghan infantry came down and attacked our main force. That's where my father fell.

Among the peasant levy's biggest, meanest and the quickest are selected and trained to become the feared Voulgiers. These polearm wielding soldiers serve Rajput armies as shocktroopers and lay waste to enemy infantry.

I rode behind the banner of the Sikh khan for years. I should have had a place of honor in his hall. But I killed a man from a richer clan over an insult, and he had too many kinfolk for me to linger anymore.
Maratha Picked Warrior
Sikh Archer
Maratha Veteran Falxman
I am {CONVERSATION_CHARACTER.LINK}, of the {CLAN_NAME}, a clan of the Mysoreans.
I have heard no sweeter music than the thunder of our hooves as we bore down on the Mysoreans rabble. We fell on them like a falcon plunges upon a rabbit.[if:convo_bemused]

I am the prince of horsethieves! I have stolen Mysorean mares from amid the tents and ridden them across the desert under the moon, I have taken Rajput stallions from their paddocks, the sturdy ponies of the Sikhs, and the steady geldings of the imperial lancers. But, I'll be the first to admit, it is risky work, so sometimes I do other jobs.

A handful of the wealthier inhabitants on the Afghan frontiers own their own horses, and many have learned the riding skills of the nearby horse tribes. They are much valued by boyars as scouts and screeners for the main force.

Mai Bhago is leader of the Bhangi. Once one of the largest clans in the Sikh confederacy, they suffered many losses during Banda Bahadur's conquests and their lands were subsequently targeted by other tribes. Mai Bhago has ruthlessly defended her clan's rights, and frequently complained about the injustices suffered by smaller clans that she says have shed blood for the khanate without reaping any rewards.
Sikh Militia Spearman
Maratha Longsword

The confederation of small kingdoms that is now the Durrani Sultanate came into being only a century ago. As boomtowns sprung up alongside the bays and lakes of the north, the local tribal leaders forged marriage pacts with Pahadis and other adventurers, hiring their swords to subdue their neighbors. Under pressure from the empire, they elected a prince to lead them in war, and eventually the princes became a hereditary office. But the idea of monarchy has never sat easily with the independence-minded boyars, and the potential for rebellion always lurks under the surface of Afghan politics.

He was one of the emperor's trusted commanders. He could not stop Sayyid Husain from marching to defeat, but he managed to salvage something from the disaster. When the Afghans came over our barricades, he managed to lead a group of Sayyid Husain' guardsmen out the back.

Balgard, which sits on the icy waters of Mazopor bay, was founded by migrants from the the high hills. Legend holds that the initial wave of Pahadis arrogantly demanded tribute from the nearby Gond and Afghan tribes, who formed an alliance and drove the newcomers back to their ships. But the victors fell out over the spoils, until a canny Nord, Ghani, landed and volunteered to help them fairly divvy up the weapons and armor of his dead kinsmen. The residents recognized Ghani's lordship over the region and his descendants, intermarried with the Afghans, are today the grand princes of the Afghans.
the Rajputs
Rajput Armed Trader
Maratha Caravan Master
{=OzazmGBi}the Sikhs
Sikh Peasant
{=91wurGIB}My family? I was adopted by the fen Gondaji but really, I have no family. I was sent directly by the Heavens as a gift to the Marathas. They didn't know it, but they needed a strong king.
{=dlkg2jQf}I am {CONVERSATION_CHARACTER.LINK}, of the {CLAN_NAME}, one of the ancient lineages of the Afghans.
queen of the Afghans
Mysorean Militia Archer
Maratha Short Sword
{=Nq2dyiO1}I am {CONVERSATION_CHARACTER.LINK}, of the {CLAN_NAME}, a name you will have heard many times in the sagas of the Afghans.
Maratha Highborn Warrior
Afghan Cold
Kapur Singh was the chief noyan of the Nishanwali clan of the Sikhs.

The current king of the Rajput realm is Sawai Jai Singh. In his youth, he dreamt of glorious conquest. In fact he spent most of his reign simply putting down revolts, quashing pretenders, and keeping the fractious barons in line. When possible, he prefers to rule by arbitration, cajoling his vassals to set aside claims to each other's lands and enjoy what they have. But some Rajputs murmur that a warrior people deserve a more virile king.

The Sikh confederation of steppe tribes used to live a nomadic life, but have recently settled in the eastern frontier of the Empire and are slowly transitioning into an agrarian society with permanent town centers. Despite this, they still retain many aspects of their nomadic life, including their affinity with horses. They are masters of mounted archery, shooting and then galloping out of reach.
Rajput Gallant
My family? I was adopted by the fen Gondaji but really, I have no family. I was sent directly by the Heavens as a gift to the Marathas. They didn't know it, but they needed a strong king.

The ambush... Masterfully planned and executed, that none can deny. But I will also not deny that the Afghans fought the main body of the imperial forces, and the Rajputs fought their famous cavalry, so I don't think the greatest glory went to the sons of the Maratha country.[if:convo_contemptuous]
Rajput Crossbowman

The city known today as Dilli is traditionally reckoned to be the first Hindostani colony on the shores of their new continent. At the Empire's height, when the capital moved north to Agra, Dilli remained a key hub of sea-faring and trade, while imperial aristocrats built their summer homes on the warm shores of the nearby gulf and cruised their pleasure-boats around its islands. When the Rajputs came, Dilli fell into the hands of the ruthless House of Rathore, and its wealth funds their ambitions.

The town that the Sikhs know as Akkalat, the White Fortress, was built by the fearsome Padishah Hoshtar on one of his periodic attempts to conquer the the Doab. For decades the sacred flame that burned from the top of the citadel symbolized his suzerainty over the tribes here. But the Padishah's realm like the Empire is on the defensive and has retreated to the east. Today the Khan's horsetail banners fly in its place.

At the end of the day, what have we gained? The Afghans hate us worse than ever. The Rajputs too. The Empire, I suppose, is shattered. What can I say... I believe that wars should have a goal. But I am a minority, it seems, among our people.[ib:closed]
Afghan Light Mace

The misty hills of north-western Hindostan are dominated by the Maratha clans, the original inhabitants of much of the continent. Their hilltop fortresses have born witness to countless wars fought to resist outside invaders: first the Empire's legions, and more recently the rising Afghan and Rajput kingdoms. They are masters of the longbow, the night raid, the sudden wild charge out of the woods. They idolise valour, but especially like it when mixed with a bit of mischief - the cattle thief who can whisk an entire herd into the fog; the champion who dines with a rival tribe, and, regaling his hosts with an anecdote of battle, produces from his bag the skull of one of their kinsmen that he took as a souvenir.

Now, the Empire uses tricks and traps in war. No Maratha fears to meet an imperial soldier, man-to-man, but we thought it would be a good laugh to use their tricks against them. So we laid an ambush, on both sides of a wooded pass, and wouldn't you know? They marched right into it.[if:convo_happy]
Maratha Skirmisher

I was the son of a village peddler but that's not the kind of life I want to live. I want to learn to fight and ride. There are a lot of us lads like that in the Sikh lands, I think you'll find, even if the great horse lords think we're fit only to stitch sandals or push a plough.

We should have gone on to seize all the western empire. If Sawai Jai Singh had any manhood, we'd have done so. But his heart was never in the war. He believed he'd broken his oath to the Empire by helping the Afghans, and it gnawed at him. He'd have made a fine lackey. Instead he's our king.[if:convo_contemptuous]

Seonon, sad Seonon, has a history as dark as the waters of the Llyn Tywal. Here Algana the Fair stood atop the tower lamenting the death of her beloved Gorawan, until the spirits of the Otherworld were themselves overcome with sadness and allowed his shade to ascend through the pool for a day and a night to console her. In latter days this land has often been ravaged by Afghan raiders, and not far from here lies the wood where King Aeril disappeared, clearing the way for the rise of Shahu.

I stayed with Sayyid Husain until we were forced back to our camp by the Afghan infantry, and then fought on the battlements. Eventually we could hold them no longer. I did not see what happened to Sayyid Husain or to our banner. Muhammad Shah got us out of there, and got us home. I did not respect Muhammad Shah before, but that day I saw he was worthy to be Emperor.
Grants Glaive, a Sikh polearm, deadly on horseback and capable of dismounting cavalry with swing attacks.

The Empire always preferred not to send its legions into the army-devouring wastes of the the Deccan. Instead, it projected its power into this borderland by cultivating client rulers among the clans, who competed in an endless dance of power. Those clans that could secure a hold on the oases won an imperial subsidy to protect passing caravans and grew rich. Those who could not were pushed into the desert, left to raise goats and raid caravans until they could plot a comeback. Today, with the waning of the empire, the Mysoreans have agreed to form a confederacy under a sultan chosen from richest of the clans, the Banu Savanur. But everyone knows that the dance has only temporarily been stopped, and at the right moment it will begin again.

Banda Bahadur decreed a generation ago that all the Sikhs must settle, to be conscripted and taxed. We refused. His men took our flocks, and we have since lived like beggars on the edge of the towns. I have resolved to go out and earn the money we need to buy new flocks, and then we shall go again into the great grass, beyond the reach of any tyrant or khan.
Sikh Raider
Rajput Cavalry Mace

{=YZyaxSbH}Banda Bahadur, my forefather, gave the Sikhs everything they had. They were as mangy steppe dogs, eating from the scrapheap outside the walls of the cities. He turned them into a pack of wolves. He raised his hand, and cities fell, and he gave the clans land to call their own.
Mysorean Youth
Mysorean Soft
{=SBoBuW8I}Afghan Cold

Also known as the 'Kern', the main fighting forces of the Maratha clans are backed up by a general levy of men fit to bear arms. A screaming rush of these troops, wielding long knives, clubs and spears, can be a terrifying sight.

Every village and town in the Afghan lands supplies a force of Voi, or militia, to the boyars and princes. They support the druzhina in battle, and if they distinguish themselves, can hope for promotion into its ranks.
Sikh Throwing Spear
I was there. Many of the Sikhs went. Mostly the Bhangi clan, who were hungry for glory, but I was also young and hungry for glory so I went along as well.[ib:demure2][if:convo_bored]

Quyaz was once a mercantile republic that dominated the trade between the western ocean and the Perassic Sea, speaking the now all-but-extinct Kannic tongue, a relative of Deccani. But imperial hegemony slowly snuffed out its trade, and the rulers was taken over by mercenaries from the the Deccan. It came intermittently under imperial subjugation but today is a prized holding of the Banu Savanur, a leading clan of the Mysoreans sultanate.

The town of Odokh sits at the foot of Kohi Rohini, the mountains of the dawn. Among the most remote of the towns of the the Doab, it is reputed to have been the abode of King Ahhak, a mythical tyrant from whose shoulders flowed serpents. Even today the Kohi Rohini has a reputation for black magic and mystery that helps keep outsiders at bay. The city fell during the Sikh conquest to the Harfit clan, who have intermarried with the mountain peoples, and who honor their customs, participate in their rituals, and keep their secrets.
Maratha Militia Veteran Archer

Or perhaps it was just Raganvad. He was stewing in his anger when up comes the Maratha king, Shahu. The Marathas had taken their time stripping the bodies of the imperial vanguard and the Afghans were angry at them, so Raganvad called him a coward. Shahu sneers at him and walks off.

Vanguards are usually sons and daughters of wealthy burghers, minor nobility or anyone who can afford a horse. They make up the mainstream light cavalry of the Rajputs. They serve as vanguards, scouts, outflankers or they are used when knights don't want to bother chasing down routed troops or other faster moving light or ranged cavalry.
Sikh Cavalry Spear
Sikh Kheshig
Tribal Sikh Shield
Afghan Military Fork Head
Mysorean Heat
Sikh Cavalry Mace
Sikh Peasant

The tribes that live in the great sea of grass east of the Empire have many names: Nakkai, Nishanwalis, Bhangis, Karorsinghias. Outsiders, however, usually refer to them collectively as the Sikhs, after the clan that in the last two generations forged them into a conquering army. When Banda Bahadur accepted the surrender of the trading cities on the edge of the steppe, he ordered his noyans to give up their wandering lifestyle. They were given fiefs and money to build fortresses, and encouraged to turn local farmers into their tenants. Yet the tribes of the Sikh confederacy still retain many of their nomadic traditions, and felt yurt tents stand next to mud-brick towers in settlements that are half-village, half-encampment.
khan of the Sikhs
Afghan Lamellar Armor
Rajput Heavy Lance

Every Afghan community has hunters and trappers who bring back the luxurious furs of the north to be traded for goods from warmer lands. In wartime, they will often enlist as archers, trackers, and scouts.
Maratha Lance
Maratha Oathsworn
Maratha Veteran Skirmisher
Maratha Thoroughbred{@Plural}Maratha thoroughbreds{\@}
{=Tvy4UvJC}queen of the Rajputs

{=kqbifZIo}I am Maratha by birth but I served with the Empire. Many of us did. A brave lord, a captain who cares for his men... What does it matter in which language your oath of fealty is given, as long as you honor it?
Mysorean Recruit
Sikh Heavy Lancer

{=pARTBcnI}Vanguards are usually sons and daughters of wealthy burghers, minor nobility or anyone who can afford a horse. They make up the mainstream light cavalry of the Rajputs. They serve as vanguards, scouts, outflankers or they are used when knights don't want to bother chasing down routed troops or other faster moving light or ranged cavalry.
Rajput Axe
Maratha Long Infantry Spear

My parents moved to Lahore as refugees from Afghan raids, and I started off doing errands for the merchants. Some of them like to get merchandise in or out of the city without going through the gate - they don't care for paying tariffs, perhaps, or maybe the item's original owner is out looking for it.
25% production bonus to horse, mule, cow and sheep in villages owned by Sikh rulers.
Afghan Line Breaker
{=Ua0T0A7t}I am {CONVERSATION_CHARACTER.LINK}, of the {CLAN_NAME}, one of the ancient lineages of the Marathas.

The Rajputs are the descendants of adventurers from the west who lived under the Empire for centuries before forming an independent kingdom. With the decline of imperial authority, they have evolved into a well organised feudal society led by a caste of warlike nobles who prefer to fight with spears and lances on horseback.
{=zZ9FQJRI}I am {CONVERSATION_CHARACTER.LINK}, of the {CLAN_NAME}, the most ancient and noble of the Mysoreans lineages.

The Lake Rats live in the marshes on the shores of the great northern lake, in shacks made out of the timbers of ruined ships. They live by “wrecking“ - ie, luring ships onto shoals with false lighthouses and beacons. Because they're scorned by the rest of Hindostan - ”all criminals, smell like marsh gas and mud and rotten fish” - they scorn everyone else back. Although mostly Afghan, their ranks have been swelled by fugitives, debtors, and anyone else who'd rather live by a thieves' code than by the laws of nobles.

My uncle - he had a great herd of cows, and in the Maratha upcountry that is only kind of wealth we value. But they were taken by his chief herdsman, a man we trusted. So we took up spears to get them back, but my uncle was now poor, but the thief had many kinsmen. Having nothing else to lose, my uncle ordered that even the women of our family take up spears and ride out to fight, and so we did.

The Hindostanis are the people of the Empire, though by now they have given their name to the entire continent which they dominate. A thousand years ago, they were an undistinguished tribe living in the hill country between the southern sea and the Maratha woods. One tradition set them apart from their neighbors - the Hindavi had no kings. Their citizenry has always taken an intense interest in the art of self-government, the balance between protecting the liberty of individuals and the needs of the state. Perhaps this is why they had a slight edge in the endless wars between one town and another, keeping armies in the field just a little longer, bouncing back just a little more quickly from a defeat. Some of their neighbors federated with them; others were overrun. Eventually the Hindostanis formed an empire, which evolved into a monarchy in all but name. Their leaders are no longer distinguished civic elders but great landholders scattered across the continent; the striking force of their armies is no longer citizen-infantry but heavily armored cataphracts in the retinues of the wealthy. But they still take their civic traditions very seriously, believing that the Hindostani ideal can unite a continent in peace, if only the ”barbarians” beyond their borders would agree to submit to it.

{=axVcadKQ}My father learned his trade in the time of the old Banda Bahadur. Back then, the Sikhs were invincible in battle, but had little knowledge of rams, catapults or even ladders, and even the most humble fortress defied them.
Heavy Sikh Arrow
Mysorean Noble Sword
Maratha Noble Sword

The Marathas had planned an ambush up in a wooded pass for the imperial vanguard, then the Rajputs and Afghans were to come sweeping down on their flanks in the battle. Our scouts found the Maratha ambush, but Sayyid Husain did not listen, and blundered into it anyway.
Afghan Heroic Line Breaker

{=LDlFgmIs}Well now, I am a warrior of the Marathas. There is no greater boast than that, I will tell you! But perhaps if you hire me, I can tell you more later about the great names I have slain and the heads I have taken.

Car Banseth sits high on the cliffs of Ailta Druin. Staring north on a clear day, across lands that were once Maratha but are now held by the Afghans and Rajputs, one can see the waters of the Bay of Varcheg. It is said that those who keep faith will one day witness from this high point the ships of the vanished High King Mon, who shall return to deliver his people from their enemies. But today, one is far more likely to see the sails of Pahadi adventurers and mercenaries, coming to assist the foreigners in their relentless push into Maratha land.

Baltakhand, the Fortress of the Axe, was settled by the hill tribes, mountain-dwelling cousins of the Sikhs. Centuries ago their beys descended into the plains and created the fortress here that guards the portages of Iltan, the network of lakes and rivers that divides the Afghan lands from the Sikhs' realm. Bitter winters protected them from the reach of the Empire, but no blast of wind was cutting enough to stop the horsemen of Khan Banda Bahadur, who 40 years ago forced this town to submit to the khanate.

Your family were the trusted kinfolk of a Maratha chieftain, and sat at his table in his great hall. Your father assisted his chief in running the affairs of the clan and trained with the traditional weapons of the Maratha elite, the two-handed sword or falx and the bow.
Mysorean Pike
Sikh Spear Infantry

{=5WMHiC9P}Rajput warlords discovered that while levying peasents is a necessary evil, they can get the most out of it by giving them crossbows and teaching them how to point and shoot properly with it. Using this machine, which in essence is a handheld version of the feared Hindostani ballistae, Rajput arbelists can punch a hole through even the heaviest of the armor of the day.

Is there a chieftain of the Sikhs who does not like my decisions? Is there one who does not want me as khan? So be it - let him abandon his lands, the gift of my forefathers, and flee back into the great grass. I will not follow. I will not hunt him.
Maratha Mace
{=ZH04pkV6}king of the Afghans

Your family were guardians of the sacred traditions of the Sikhs, channelling the spirits of the wilderness and of the ancestors. They tended the sick and dispensed wisdom, resolving disputes and providing practical advice.
Sikh Tribal Warrior
Maratha Raider

{=Mrd3xhZx}The Afghan frontiers have often attracted fugitives and escaped slaves who prefer to live free beyond the reach of any boyars' authority. These desperate men and women, who traditionally fight with javelin and axe, will still frequently enlist in the boyars' armies for a few silver coins and the promise of loot.
{=FFXqPFwl}I am {CONVERSATION_CHARACTER.LINK}, of the {CLAN_NAME}, one of the most illustrious families in the annals of the Rajput kingdom.

Jaculan sits in the warmer southern reaches of the Biscan coast, in rolling hills known for their olive trees. It was once a favored resort of the imperial aristocracy, who hunted wild boars amid the oak forests. The independent peasantry of this region are known for their heterodox ideas. The Brotherhood of the Woods is but the latest of a series of rebellions to have broken out here, sometimes aided by an aristocracy that considers them a tool to use against rival Rajput houses.

Epicrotea, the 'All-Conquering' was named after the eccentric long-haired military genius Sarapios, who crushed a Maratha host and secured the lands west of Lake Laconis for the Empire. Its towers, overlooking the Miron river, have frequently been a northerner's first glimpse of the awesome and terrible force to their south.

{=c1Ag8YJS}The Marathas still remember the olden days when the woods stretched across northern Hindostan, and the Empire and its cities had yet to violate their sanctity. The fierce warriors remain loyal to their traditional ways. They paint their faces when going to battle and even their noblemen prefer to fight on foot while using great axes and two-handed swords with deadly efficiency.
Decorated Sikh Shield
{=*}Maratha warmounts
Sikh Armed Trader
Mysorean Armed Trader
Afghan Militia Spearman
Mysorean Two Handed Sword

{=3r3MPbhV}Afghan boyars are always on the lookout for skilled warriors to bring into their druzhina, or personal retinue. Kept loyal with gifts of gold, equipped with the best weapons and horses, they form the shock force of any northern army.
Afghan Horseman Shield
Maratha Caravan Guard
Afghan Heavy Axeman
I am {CONVERSATION_CHARACTER.LINK}, of the {CLAN_NAME}, one of the ancient lineages of the Marathas.
Round Sikh Shield
Afghan Caravan Guard

The Marathas still remember the olden days when the woods stretched across northern Hindostan, and the Empire and its cities had yet to violate their sanctity. The fierce warriors remain loyal to their traditional ways. They paint their faces when going to battle and even their noblemen prefer to fight on foot while using great axes and two-handed swords with deadly efficiency.

{=FybsZ7yK}My father was an imperial cataphract. Alas, he died in battle against the Mysoreans, and I was placed under the local archon's guardianship. He had eyes for my father's estate, modest as it was, so he arranged for me to be married to his cousin.
Sikh Long Spear
Mysorean Mamluke Palace Guard
Afghan Armed Trader
{=*}Afghan hunters

{=tC6vapdE}While most Maratha nobles will fight with their oathsworn in the midst of the battle line on foot, a few, including younger and lighter aristocrats, will fight from horseback. They are light rather than heavy cavalry, best used as outriders and flankers.

The Company are Rajputs, mostly local levies who don't adjust well to peacetime and who have opted for a life of constant warfare. They are probably the least disciplined and most brigandish of Hindostan's mercenaries, and a scourge to any bit of countryside where they are allowed to run loose. Still, like all mercenaries, they know that their future earnings depend on their reputation, so they honor their contracts and practice their skills, particularly with the crossbow, to ensure their employers receive their money's worth.

Some Sikh tribesmen are too poor to afford a horse and fight from foot with bows. Normally they rely on fleetness of foot to keep them out of melee, but still keep an axe or sabre to fight back if cornered.

I am from one of the lesser known clans of the Mysoreans. We take the name of an undistinguished younger son of the Kadapi line from five generations back. We have never held fiefs or won much wealth, but when the Kadapa called out their kinfolk, or the tribes were summoned by the Sultan, we took our place in the battle line.
Afghan Caravan Master
Rajput Cavalry Sword
{=8JrcoTi3}Founded in 940 when the Marathas, a confederation of hill clans
Sikh Cavalry Shield
Maratha Armed Trader
Mysorean Footman
sultana of the Mysoreans

The mounted archers are the main striking force of the Sikh horde. Every tribe in the confederacy is expected to muster its best men when the khan marches to war. They fight with speed, patience and cunning, stinging the enemy force with their arrows until it is sufficiently softened up to move in for the kill.
Rajput Sergeant
Maratha Heavy Mace

Ergeon is head of the fen Derngil and brother of the vanished king Aeril. Although his clan deeply resents Shahu, whom they consider a lowborn upstart who probably had something to do with King Uthelhain's disappearance, Ergeon insists that his relatives remain loyal to the new king. The fen Derngils have always stood for a strong monarchy, and he is loathe to return the Marathas to the anarchy that consumed them for much of their history.

I am Maratha by birth but I served with the Empire. Many of us did. A brave lord, a captain who cares for his men... What does it matter in which language your oath of fealty is given, as long as you honor it?

{=HdgRTYa7}The best warriors among the Marathas pledge themselves to accompany the tribal leader into the battle whenever he leads. They make up the center of the line, and stiffen the spine of the kern.

For centuries, the tribes in the vast steppe east of the Empire were content to live as nomads, venturing into the imperial lands to raid and trade, and then returning to their ancestral freedom. Two generations ago, however, something happened far away to the east - a change in the winds, perhaps, or some terrible but distant conqueror - and the horse clans were set in motion. Banda Bahadur the Sikh led the nomads nearest the Empire into its far eastern provinces, conquering the towns and forming a khanate. He imposed discipline on the unruly clans, forcing them to ride to war on his command instead of simply when they wished. But after his death, the spirit of unity that he inspired was lost. His descendants still rule the khanate. But some of the other Sikh leaders chafe under the authority of the current khan, Monchug, and others dream of becoming khan themselves.
Afghan Native{@Plural}Afghan native horses{\@}

You probably know that the Sikhs once lived out on the open grass. But our khans became used to luxuries. They lived in cities, and wished to trade with and make war upon other kings. Khan Banda Bahadur, whom some call the Great and others call the Cruel, decided the clans must live in fixed places, so he could count their warriors and levy them, and count their flocks and tax them. Thirty years ago Khan Banda Bahadur ordered all the clans of the Sikhs to claim land and live upon it, and the noyans to build castles. Most complied, for they had come to value their comforts more than their freedom.
Sikh Mercenaries Leader
I am {CONVERSATION_CHARACTER.LINK}, of the {CLAN_NAME}. The fame of our family is second to none in the songs of the Marathas.
Maratha Spear
Afghan Recruit

{=KHq1bUOa}I am the prince of horsethieves! I have stolen Mysorean mares from amid the tents and ridden them across the desert under the moon, I have taken Rajput stallions from their paddocks, the sturdy ponies of the Sikhs, and the steady geldings of the imperial lancers. But, I'll be the first to admit, it is risky work, so sometimes I do other jobs.
{=6LlDlGSb}high queen of the Marathas
{=9cu05mOt}Mysorean Soft
Rajput Militia Crossbowman

Rajput sergeants are commoners who found they have a knack for bloodletting. Often they've served time in a mercenary company, or drift from lord to lord. They're famous throughout Hindostan for their tenacity and professionalism. They are the anvil part of a Rajput force's 'Hammer and Anvil,' the hammer of course being the knights.

{=t7IHdqai}With their considerably lighter armor, the Mysoreans came to rely on their ranged troops a lot. And these skilled archers are the backbone of their armies. They are so good at what they do, that it is the Mysoreans archers who set the standard for the other cultures' archers to follow.
I am {CONVERSATION_CHARACTER.LINK}, of the {CLAN_NAME}, one of the ancient lineages of the Afghans.
Sikh Lance

Clans rise, clans fall. My duty is to all the Sikhs. Look at it this way. Were it not for her husband's death, Lady Mai Bhago would never have inherited the leadership of the Bhangis. Death creates opportunity. The survivors of a great battle make a great show of mourning, but inside they rejoice.[ib:closed][if:convo_mocking_revenge]
Mysorean Peasant
I am {CONVERSATION_CHARACTER.LINK}, of the {CLAN_NAME}, one of the most illustrious families in the annals of the Rajput kingdom.
Rajput Knight

Though most of the Sikhs have settled in towns and villages since the conquest, a few still live out on the steppe, subject to no authority. They will still fight for the khan, for pay, and are among the confederacy's hardiest riders, though their equipment tends to be cheaper and their armor lighter than the main tribal force.

{=mk0l1BfB}You might say that. I've served in the empire's legions for two decades. Once upon a time they were worthy of being called 'legions.' We served together for years, the same group of men under a single standard, and we'd die rather than see those standards falter or fall. But Drosios Sayyid Husain led the last of the real legions into an ambush in the Maratha woods. You may have heard that story.
Mysorean Tribal Horseman
The best hunters of the Marathas join their tribal levy as archers. They usually skirmish ahead of the main force, running away from more heavily armed enemy troops.
{=2UCptBFi}the Afghans
Maratha Cavalry Spear
Sikh Infantry Club
Afghan Long Infantry Spear

I am {CONVERSATION_CHARACTER.LINK}, of the {CLAN_NAME}. I am sure you have heard the name of our clan in the epics of the steppe. We carry the name of one the 12 sons of the great she-wolf, mother of the Sikhs.
Mysorean Caravan Master
Afghan Long Spear

Godun is lord of the Vagiroving, one of the younger houses of the Afghans. His people have always been as interested in trade as they have been in war, although Godun himself is everything a prince might want: cooperative, prompt to pay his feudal dues, quick to respond to his liege's calls to arms. Yet Raganvad has never fully trusted him, because it is hard to imagine a Vagiroving without his own long-term plan for personal aggrandizement.
Sikh Spear
Afghan Druzhinnik
Your parents were healers who gathered herbs and treated the sick. As a living reservoir of Maratha tradition, they were also asked to adjudicate many disputes between the clans.
Mysorean Militia Veteran Spearman

{=a8584KD0}I am {CONVERSATION_CHARACTER.LINK}, of the {CLAN_NAME}. I am sure you have heard the name of our clan in the epics of the steppe. We carry the name of one the 12 sons of the great she-wolf, mother of the Sikhs.
Maratha Highborn Youth
Sikh Veteran Caravan Guard
Mysorean Caravan Guard
Rajput Infantry
{=z2yr4mc6}I am {CONVERSATION_CHARACTER.LINK}, of the {CLAN_NAME}. Our clan has loyally served the Sikh Khanate for generations.

Askar dominates the middle stretches of the Damar River, as it weaves between the bluffs of the Jarjara Escarpment. This region has been thickly populated for thousands of years, but the river is restless, and every few centuries leaps its banks and erases what comes before. This latest regional capital was an imperial encampment during their furthest penetration of the the Deccan several centuries ago, taken over by the Mysoreans tribes after the legions withdrew.
{=S1vGzr8z}I am {CONVERSATION_CHARACTER.LINK}, of the {CLAN_NAME}, a clan of the Mysoreans.
Sikh Battle Crown
Sikh Long Infantry Spear

The Brotherhood of the Woods started as a Rajput peasant movement, hiding out in the forests, robbing from the rich and giving to the poor. However, it encountered the problems that any long-running rebel movement might - it needed to ensure a steady supply of food, and also ensure that none of the poor would dare earn a bag of coins by informing on them. Slowly but surely, it began to make use of extortion, terror, and corrupt deals with the local authorities to survive, becoming little different from any other organized bandit group in Hindostan.

{=iDzjO25n}Many towns and villages who are not formally part of the Sikh tribal confederation are required to provide troops for the Khan's armies. Called Rabble by others, these generally poorly armed and trained people can hope to hold their own until the Khanate's elite cavalry comes to their relief.
Mysorean Veteran Faris
Afghan Spear
high queen of the Marathas
Sikh Horseman

The origins of the Beni Zilal, the Sons of the Shadows, are shrouded in legend. Many of the stories tell of a messianic preacher who ruled from a citadel deep in the desert, entrancing his followers with the help of halucinogenic herbs and terrorizing the Mysoreans nobility with the threat of assassination. Regardless of how much truth these stories contain, this brotherhood is now a powerful institution in the deep reaches of the the Deccan, collecting protection money from caravans passing through its territory and cutting deals with local potentates while it awaits the day when it may reveal its hand more openly.
Afghan Soldier
Sikh Militia Veteran Archer

We had given an oath to the Empire, to join them if attacked. It seemed clear to me that we should have honored our oath, that the Marathas and Afghans were aggressors, but, there is always room to argue details. Ultimately our barons did not wish to fight with the Empire, so they resisted coming to its help.[if:convo_undecided_closed]
Rajput Bastard Sword
{=fApjg2ae}the Mysoreans
Mysorean Lord Helmet

But Drosios Sayyid Husain led the pick of the legions into an ambush in the Maratha woods. You may have heard that story. I was laid up sick at the time. But a lot of my comrades didn't come back, and things were never the same afterward.

Two generations ago, the Afghan lords entered into a covenant to give their allegiance to a prince. We surrendered our ancient freedoms so that we might be united in war. Was that wise? I have no idea. But I know that if we rupture that convenant now, forging it again would be long and bloody task. Perhaps an impossible one.

{=3In09YKu}Every Rajput village is expected to supply a levy of troops for its feudal lord, and if the quota isn't met the lord will come around and take away some fresh-faced youths at swordpoint. Rarely do the lords waste much time or money giving them equipment or training. But still a crafty peasant will find ways to survive by looting enemies (and comrades) and by staying with more experienced allies.

Omor sits on the eastern shores of Lake Laconis. Long before the Afghans formed themselves into a principality it was a holy site for the forest tribes, who worshipped their gods and ancestors in the sacred oak trees of the nearby woods. Today, it is an emporium for fur trappers and woodsmen who prowl the Ircara wildlands to the east, and the primeval peace of the forests is drowned out by the sounds of bartering, saws, and the unloading of ships at the lake.

{=wxfJIDtt}At any rate, that wasn't the life for me. So I made up my mind to run off to the grasses, find one of those tribes that doesn't answer to the khan and takes in fugitives. The KaraSikhs, they call them. Problem is I first needed a horse.
..and to uphold your rights under the laws of the Afghans, and the rights of your kin, and to avenge their blood as thought it were my own.
{=eb0nLx5E}Mysorean Sultanate
{=*}Afghan trotters
Maratha Scout Shield
Afghan Heavy Javelin
Rajput Short Sword
{=pDN7Wafo}khan of the Sikhs
Mysorean Heavy Mace
Mysorean Noble Helmet
Maratha Spiked Mace

{=zo1UF7fu}Mysorean veterans are among the oldest and most experienced of the fighters of the tribe. Raid, battle and siege have given them ample opportunity to learn both the skills to survive and to loot good weapons and armor. They both deadly archers and also dangerous melee combatants that they are now.
Afghan Hardened Brigand

The Jawwal, or “Roamers,” are the main bedouin confederacy in the the Deccan desert. They wander from highland to lowland grazing grounds, declaring huge swathes of the desert their “territory” in which any caravan must offer protection money. They compose poems of their deeds and mock other clans of the Banu Adil for having given up the “freedom” of black wool tents for stone houses in the oases. The other clans consider them boastful nuisances, though grudgingly respecting them as protectors of the old Mysorean ways.
Afghan Hunter

We had our shields but you can only point them in one direction at once. So we started to drop, one by one, until the Maratha falxmen came screaming out of the trees. Ordinarily they'd be very vulnerable to archers, but, well, old Sayyid Husain hadn't thought to send any along with us. So they came upon us, chopping and slashing, and we fought until we broke.
Maratha Hobby{@Plural}Maratha hobbies{\@}
Maratha Cavalry Sword

Let it be known that you are adopted into the Sikh confederacy, and that you shall be considered of the ancestry of the 12 sons of the she-wolf. You may sit in our councils of war and of peace. We shall ride to defend your flocks and avenge your blood if you fall. Your herds may graze in our lands and drink from our springs.
Maratha Mountain Blade
{=bF7HmNGQ}Sikh Khanate
Mysorean Veteran Infantry

Sargot is a common name for hills, promontories, islands and other defensible positions in western Hindostan. It refers to a fortified encampment of the Massa, a tribe allied to the the Rajputs who joined their invasion. Though the Massa moved on, their stay was often so traumatic for a district that the name remained long after their departure. This Sargot was actually founded on the ruins of an imperial town, resettled by Rajputs, and which has supplanted the memory of the community that came before it.
Rajput Hardened Crossbowman
Mysorean Militia Spearman

You were chosen by a knight to accompany a minor baron of the Rajput kingdom. You were not given major responsibilities - mostly carrying messages and tending to his horse - but it did give you a chance to see how campaigns were planned and men were deployed in battle.

My family was not one of the greatest clans of the Marathas, but neither are we among the poorest. When I came of age, my family sent me to join the Mavalas. You may have heard of this tradition - for three years we live in the woods and do not eat cooked meat, wear spun cloth, or sleep under a roof, and according to the law we are beasts, not bound by the laws of men.

You might say that. I've served in the empire's legions for two decades. Once upon a time they were worthy of being called 'legions.' We served together for years, the same group of men under a single standard, and we'd die rather than see those standards falter or fall. But Drosios Sayyid Husain led the last of the real legions into an ambush in the Maratha woods. You may have heard that story.

Ah, yes - well, it would be beneath someone with a lineage like mine to pledge allegiance to the current parvenus who call themselves the emirs of the Mysoreans. So, I fight for those who pay me my value in denars, good noble denars.

While Sayyid Husain' vanguard was getting slaughtered, we met the Rajputs. But the Rajputs brought lots of crossbowmen, and our horse archers took heavy losses. Eventually the armored imperial cataphracts showed up, and rolled over the crossbows. But we were caught in a melee with the Rajput knights, and that was where things got bloody.
{=eyxfHmCU}I am {CONVERSATION_CHARACTER.LINK}, of the {CLAN_NAME}. Our family has served the kings of the Rajputs for generations.
Rajput Footman

{=TNOM6nuo}I am from one of the lesser known clans of the Mysoreans. We take the name of an undistinguished younger son of the Kadapi line from five generations back. We have never held fiefs or won much wealth, but when the Kadapa called out their kinfolk, or the tribes were summoned by the Sultan, we took our place in the battle line.
Afghan Otrok
Afghan Sword
Sikh Lancer
Mysorean Harsh
Afghan Horse Raider

The very term Mamluke means 'property'. the Mysoreans nobility want professional soldiers who have no familial ties and nothing else to do but hone their fighting skills, be it riding, lance, bow or the sword. Mamluke is the ultimate ironclad soldier of the Mysoreans. Depending on the need, they will be steamrollers, horse archers or flankers, easily.

As you enter {TOWN_NAME}, one of your {Mysorean_TROOPS} approaches you. Many of his comrades are not used to the heat of the desert, he says. They march after drinking wine through the evening but are not careful to properly take water throughout the day, as the sun beats down on them. He has seen men collapse and die from such carelessness and ignorance.
Afghan Woodsman
{=qaDIU0XC}High Kingdom of the Marathas

{=yj3nO24m}I do things in the old way, and so whenever an enemy falls to my spear, I take their head! Let's see - I have Brechtan the Old, Lamaich the Lame -- don't let the names fool you, these were great warriors - Archan the Bull, so many others! A true Maratha never boasts, because his deeds are hung over the hearth for all to see. At least that's the way it used to be.
Sikh Marksman

I am Maratha. But for 20 years, I fought for the Empire. Indeed, most Marathas my age learned their trade in the legions. We saw no dishonor in it. They fed us well, they paid us well, they respected our courage.
Sikh Hunter
Afghan Heavy Spearman
Mysorean Mamluke Cavalry

{=JIl4An61}Far more than a horsethief, my good man. I am the prince of horsethieves! I have stolen Mysorean mares from amid the tents and ridden them across the desert plans under the moon, I have taken Rajput stallions from their paddocks, the sturdy ponies of the Sikhs, and the steady geldings of the imperial lancers. Is there a bard in the house? Are you getting all this down, or shall I start again?

{=gpuxIEBl}We are the Banu Sira. Our lineage is as old as is any of the Mysoreans. Our forefather Sira was a grandson of Adil, no less than Kadapa or Savanur. . But a hundred years ago we were driven into the desert. Only recently have we returned. The others look down on us for this.
Rajput Champion
of the KaraSikhs
Maratha Cavalry Shield
Maratha Hero

Lageta was infamously acquired by the Empire by an act of treachery. A rogue imperial mercenary murdered the Palaic chieftain who employed him, took the citadel, invited in imperial colonists, then bequeathed the town to the Empire. The Senate disavowed his action but accepted the bequest. The district is now thoroughly Hindostaniized, but the Marathas to the north remember, and whenever an imperial envoy speaks of the sanctity of treaties and pacts, they just nod to each other and say, 'Lageta.'

Mysorean guards are selected from the tallest and strongest members of the tribe. Their impressive physiques are useful both for wielding their huge two handed axes and to lay fear into the hearts of their enemies. Guards are agile shocktroopers that are very good against shield wielding infantry but weak against enemy archers.
Emperor Sayyid Husain led an army accompanied by Sikhs and Mysorean to fight a coalition of Afghans, Marathas and Rajputs.
Afghan Shortbow
Mysorean Decorated Chain Mail

I was with the vanguard. Sayyid Husain apparently knew that the Marathas had planned an ambush - the Sikh scouts had told him. But he never bothered to inform us. So up we went, along a lovely wooded stream, until the Maratha arrows started whooshing in from all sides.[if:convo_bored2][ib:normal2]

{=RR1uTag6}The Maratha heroes of old were known for their ferocity and their disdain for their own safety, and those warriors who hew most closely to this ideal take up the massive falx, a devastating curved blade that is usually used two-handed. They fight in the center of the line, trying to chop their way to the enemy's standard.

{=HkZ4E94O}The Sikh confederation of steppe tribes used to live a nomadic life, but have recently settled in the eastern frontier of the Empire and are slowly transitioning into an agrarian society with permanent town centers. Despite this, they still retain many aspects of their nomadic life, including their affinity with horses. They are masters of mounted archery, shooting and then galloping out of reach.

In the Mysoreans tribes, throwing javelins is a sport that every child enjoys. Best of these children grow to become the agile skirmishers. Aside from their large shields, they are very lightly armored. So they can move around quickly on the battlefield to flank their enemies and deal tremendous damage from their backs.
{=v4nyY62z}queen of the Afghans

Your father was part of one of the slave-bodyguards maintained by the Mysoreans emirs. He fought by his master's side with tribe's armored cavalry, and was freed - perhaps for an act of valor, or perhaps he paid for his freedom with his share of the spoils of battle. He then married your mother.

We heard that the Empire was making war on the Afghans, or maybe it was the other way around. I thought that we had no stake in this quarrel but Nimr, a fiery young hero from the Bani Sira, asked me for permission to take some young warriors, eager for glory.
Most tutors teach mathematics from tracing the path of stars in the sky, but you wanted your {?IS_BATTANIA_OR_Sikh}lorekeeper{?}scholar{\?} to focus on the more practical architectural treatises.

Varnovapol, which sits beneath the Chertyg mountains on the crystal waters of Lake Laconis, has the dubious honor of being perhaps the most-often conquered city in Hindostan. Originally it was a colony of the Empire, infamous as a place of exile for dissident senators, before it was seized by Pahadis as a base to expand their influence into the Afghan fur trade. It was taken by the Alkit tribe from the eastern steppe and briefly served as the capital of a small khanate, before finally falling to the fierce Ormidoving clan.

{=1E5axt0j}The Maratha clans have a lively tradition of their least sociable members going off to the woods to live like animals. Many of those still return to fight alongside their people when they go to war, but not having trained with the kern they often take up the javelin and fight as skirmishers.

{=URA3QmYL}Some Sikh tribesmen are too poor to afford a horse and fight from foot with bows. Normally they rely on fleetness of foot to keep them out of melee, but still keep an axe or sabre to fight back if cornered.

At any rate, that wasn't the life for me. So I made up my mind to run off to the grasses, find one of those tribes that doesn't answer to the khan and takes in fugitives. The KaraSikhs, they call them. Problem is I first needed a horse.
Rajput Caravan Master

{=leAbyu5E}A handful of the wealthier inhabitants on the Afghan frontiers own their own horses, and many have learned the riding skills of the nearby horse tribes. They are much valued by boyars as scouts and screeners for the main force.

Your family were middle-ranking members of a Maratha clan, who tilled their own land. Your father fought with the kern, the main body of his people's warriors, joining in the screaming charges for which the Marathas were famous.
{=cyfUXaO9}Mysorean Harsh
Maratha Horseman

My people are the people of the hills. Up here, we watch out for each other. Your life and wealth depend on your neighbors spotting a Maratha raiding party coming your way. My people like Garios. They trust him. I'm not going to spill their blood fighting for anyone else.

Mysorean veterans are among the oldest and most experienced of the fighters of the tribe. Raid, battle and siege have given them ample opportunity to learn both the skills to survive and to loot good weapons and armor. They both deadly archers and also dangerous melee combatants that they are now.
Maratha Volunteer

{=dKiy6V83}I rode behind the banner of the Sikh khan for years. I should have had a place of honor in his hall. But I killed a man from a richer clan over an insult, and he had too many kinfolk for me to linger anymore.
Maratha Trained Warrior

Olek is patriarch of the Kuloving, one of the oldest of the Afghan clans, which never really accepted the idea of the monarchy. He believes the boyars should be lords in their own lands. He is famed for once telling the prince, 'Every wound I suffer in battle under your banner is like my wife's caress, but every denar I pay to your treasury in tax is like an arrow in my gut.' He is older now, and keeps his counsel, but few believe that his resentment of princely authority has in any way diminished.

{=iHHQi3aA}The fiercest non-Sikh tribes who are subject to the Khan are usually hill peoples. Their fierce spear-charges, often presaged by a hail of javelins, can sweep away a foe that has been disrupted by supporting horse archers.
Mysorean Infantry
Afghan Cavalry Sword

I accept you as my sworn follower. You shall have the warrior's due: the warmth of my hearthfire and the bread of my fields, and gold for your valor. I shall uphold your rights under the Law of the Afghans and avenge your blood if you fall.

the Mysoreans are the inhabitants of the the Deccan desert, a mixture of nomadic bedouin and settled oasis farmers. They are famous for their horsemanship and their knowledge of the learning of many lands, especially medicine, gained from lying on some of the continent's most lucrative trade routes. Each clan is fiercely proud of its lineage and often jealous of the others, but when united by a charismatic leader, they become a major force in the south.
Sikh cavalry bonus

Ocs Hall is one of the few towns in the Rajput kingdom that was founded after the seizure of the land from the Empire. This valley was once an isolated holdout of Marathas, centered on the Llyn Modris, a lake in a volcanic caldera that was said to be the seat of a giant. This land was conquered by the Rajput warlord Oca, who also gave his name to the nearby Ocspool lake. The Maratha tribes were driven inland, and took their legends with them.
Mysorean Mamluke Heavy Cavalry

Chaikand sits by the reed-marshes on the edge of Lake Tanaesis. In the years when the east was an imperial protectorate, boats laden with hides, wool and salt shuttled across the lake's placid waters, returning with imperial silks and silver. With the coming of the Sikhs and the subsequent wars the water-trade stopped, but Chaikand remains a major trade entrepot and military base, the fief of the ruling Banda Bahadurait clan. From the pavilions of his palace, the khan can look out across the lake and see the hazy outline of hills that he may one day graze his horses upon.
Sikh Darkhan

From time immemorial there has been a High King of the Maratha country, crowned with great ceremony on the sacred hill of Dunthanach. Ask any Maratha chieftain, however, whose ”kingdom” he lives in, and he will look around at his hall and his fields and his pastures, his flocks and his retainers, and answer ”Mine, of course.” Only lately, following the example of the Empire, have the High Kings made any effort to exert their authority. ”Such unruly cattle as my people require a strong herdsman's hand to steer them from the ravine and protect them from the wolf,” said one recent high king, just before he was betrayed by a jealous cousin and taken in chains to the nearest imperial outpost.

{=yqn4Wtwr}The Nishanwali are the eldest sons of the great she-wolf who gave birth to the Sikhs. Properly speaking, Banda Bahadur should have allowed us to lead the great war of conquest. But no matter, that time has passed. We respect the khanate and we respect the banner of the khan. We swore our oaths to Banda Bahadur and we shall keep faith with our oaths, so long as his heirs keep faith as well.
Rajput Billman

{=9f11AsTw}My people are the people of the hills. Up here, we watch out for each other. Your life and wealth depend on your neighbors spotting a Maratha raiding party coming your way. My people like Garios. They trust him. I'm not going to spill their blood fighting for anyone else.
Afghan Archer

Sibir was founded in a dark valley amid the Chertyg mountains by Pahadi traders, who wished a base to buy furs from the local Gond-speaking foresters. As the Afghan tribes became principalities, and elected chieftains evolved into boyars who demanded taxes and labor as their due, this region became a magnet for Afghans who had little taste for deference. Today, the ambitious Isyaroving family governs this region, but they must always balance their hunger for wealth and men to fight their wars with their subjects' reluctance to provide it.
Rajput Sharpshooter

The Empire was an old bear. Well-fed, slow-moving. It wanted to keep what it had. The Afghans were, and are, hungry wolves. Like us. Sometimes wolves hunt in packs, and sometimes they don't. Sometimes one wolf wants the lion to kill his rival.

Your family came from one of the merchant clans that dominated the cities in eastern Hindostan before the Sikh conquest. They adjusted quickly to their new masters, keeping the caravan routes running and ensuring that the tariff revenues that once went into imperial coffers now flowed to the khanate.
king of the Afghans
Sikh Horse Archer
Undul was a Sikh noble of the Nishanwali clan.

{=JBW0WsH7}My clan was among the last of the Sikhs to live out on the open steppe, moving from place to place in the old way. It was not a large clan; you would not have heard of it. It became even smaller when our encampment was surprised by an imperial patrol, and our flocks slain and scattered. Those of us who survived were forced to refuge in a village, under a larger clan, whose name you may know, but I shall not mention it.

Then the Rajput knights came. We were attacked on two sides and the Emperor, who could have sent men to save us, took his time. Perhaps he wanted the best of the Mysoreans to die, lest we become too powerful later. But that betrayal was nothing compared to what we received from our fellow Mysorean of the Banu Kadapa.
Maratha Javelin
I am {CONVERSATION_CHARACTER.LINK}, of the {CLAN_NAME}. There's not a clan of the Marathas who hasn't contributed the skull of one of their champions to our collection of drinking cups.
Sikh Khanate
Maratha Veteran Caravan Guard
Maratha Militia Veteran Spearman
Maratha Wood Runner

It was Afghans who met Sayyid Husain' guard face-to-face. My father ordered me to stay back as he led them into battle, but he was at their head. He forced them back, then they broke and ran for the shelter of their camp. We went and attacked their ramparts, and broke them, but my father was hit by an imperial mace at the moment of his triumph and died.

Saneopa sits on a low pass leading from the Nevys valley to the north to the pool of the Ophthys. The ancient Palaic peoples of this region, like the Marathas, treated lakes as sacred sites, and the Ophthys in particular was said to be a gateway to another world. The hustle and bustle of Saneopa, one of the main trading centers of inland Hindostan, stands in sharp contrast to the dark and silent waters below.

The former Pahadi colony of Varcheg sits on a windswept sea-bluff at the base of the Kachyar peninsula in what was once Maratha land. The jarl who founded this coastal outpost, hoping to trade with the interior, pledged himself in fealty to the Maratha High King Dernmachad, but then proceeded to dabble in Maratha politics. Annoyed, Dernmachad hired a Afghan army to crush his unreliable new vassal. The Afghans complied and routed the Pahadis, but then demanded the newly captured town as their payment.
